figures.caption: Figure 1: Given the same API specification, an LLM agent produces a functional codebase under no constraints (top), but performance degrades with structural constraints (bottom).
Table 2: Assertion pass rate A% and pass@1 across constraint levels. L0 = baseline; L1–L3 impose increasingly specific structural requirements. * = evaluated on task subset.
Table 7: Per-category precision, recall, and F1 (%) of the GPT-5.2 judge against human annotations on the 50-sample validation set. Cohen’s κ = 0.975; overall accuracy = 98%.

Table 6: Subset representativeness: A% on the full 80-task set vs. the 16-task subset for each of the six validation configurations (Devstral-Small excluded). ∆ = full−subset. MAE = mean absolute difference. Pearson r = 0.976, Spearman ρ = 0.948 (N = 24, p < 10−12).
Table 4: Assertion pass rate A% by framework, aggregated across constraint levels. M = MiniSWE-Agent; O = OpenHands.
Table 10: Source repositories for feature implementation tasks. All implement the full RealWorld Conduit API with PostgreSQL, an ORM (Prisma or SQLAlchemy), and a layered architecture.

Figure 3: Example feature implementation prompt. The agent receives the full OpenAPI specification followed by a single instruction. The repository has been pre-stripped of 245 lines across 10 files (the entire comments vertical: route registrations, controller, Prisma schema entries, and validation middleware).
Figure 2: Generation prompt for an L3 task. The full OpenAPI specification (∼500 lines) is prepended verbatim but omitted here for space.
Table 5: Failure taxonomy for Qwen3-Coder-Next and MiniMax-M2.5 with Mini-SWEAgent. Coarse categories (left) show % of failed runs; subcategories for logic errors only.
Table 11: Test suite folder breakdown. The third folder accounts for 73% of all assertions because it exercises the full article lifecycle including nested resources.
Table 13: Average input and output tokens per run by constraint level and agent–model pair (K = ×103, rounded). * = 16-task subset.
Table 1: Constraint levels and task variants. WF = Web Framework (always active), Arch = Clean Architecture, PG = PostgreSQL. Variants within a level are separated by |.

Table 8: A% with and without verifier enforcement. “A%” zeros out assertion scores for runs that violate structural constraints; “Raw A%” uses behavioral-test scores only. ∆ = Raw A% − A%. Full-set models use all 80 tasks; * = 16-task subset.
